content.question: To what degree would delaying the 6/8-year track entry from Grade 5/7 to age 16 (as in Finland or Poland's 1999 reform) reduce ethnic segregation and socioeconomic inequality in Hungary, versus merely postponing existing sorting processes while ethnic-segregation mechanisms (housing, school reputation, cultural barriers to mixed-classroom interaction) persist?
content.reduction_path: Comparative ethnographic study of schools in Hungary before and after a hypothetical tracking-age delay, measuring not only composition change but also pedagogical adaptation, teacher expectations of Roma/non-Roma students, and within-classroom peer interaction. International comparison to equivalent ethnographic data from Poland post-1999 or Finland post-1972 would test whether de-tracking alone affects ethnic-minority student outcomes without complementary curriculum or teacher-training reform.
